headlines.title: INCREASE IN RESERVOIR FORCE IS NOT FEASIBLE
headlines.subtitle: Superintendent Explains Situ- ation to City Officials Low b. Bid on Clearing .!
headlines.fullText: No more men can be used advanta- geously in the reservoir work with the present materiais, Finance Commis- sioner Smith said Saturday night aft er a visit by city officiala to Lake Worth Saturday. Superintendent Lord, Smith Raym, told him that after publication of a plan to increase the force in order to rush the work, many applied, but could not use them to advantage .. The board of engineers reported m Mayor Mllam Thursday that at, the Presenta una gelnher ningerne her, and they believed It should be finished by May L. Mayor Milam is hopeful of making a remaining reservoir land. Saturday morning he received by mail an offer below the lowest bld rejected Tues- dny. The amount is not made pub- Ile by Mayor Milam in fairness to the, two men who made the offer jointly.

headlines.title: BROCK'S OFFICE SHOWS GOOD PROFIT FOR YEAR
headlines.subtitle: District Clerk Will Turn $8,277 Into Treasury-More to Be Collected.
headlines.fullText: The year closing Nov. 20, 1912, was the most prosperous for the district clerk's office in its history, according pared by District Clerk E. J. Broek. He will turn into the county treasury the sum of $8,277.32. Only $5.400.71 of this amount. how- ver represents excess fees for the fiscal year the reminder und the county's share of fees collected this your PR a year there yet remains $7,17220 of uncol- lected fees for the year to be collected during the ensuing year. According to Brock's report the of- fice earnings were $:1,652.32 during the year, while the total expenditures were only 18.281.23 during the year. In: cluding the district clerk's stated sal- ary. The total collections amounted 0 $14.681.93. Brock claims the distinction of be- ing the only district clerk who has turned in any excess fees to the county treasury ance the fee bill went into Thing term and has had an excess for third Hi- - xe === fees for 1909 were $2.007.68: 1910, $897.19; 1911, $5,505,47. 915 mot obtainable as County Auditor Mul- holland has not finished checking it.

headlines.title: KENTUCKY AVENUE CHURCH IS WINNER
headlines.subtitle: Meeting of City Federation of Baptist Young People's Union Is Held.
headlines.fullText: The Kentucky Avenue Baptist Young People's Union is the banner umion of the city for the month of December erdem of the R. T. P. U. was held SThisy night at the First Baptist Church, North Side. The averages, of which the Ken- tueky Avenue Society attained high- tucky Avenne, society, mien- matie Bible reading, attendance of master and general Interest in the work. Sting Jemele MeNell is superintendent of the Kentucky Avenue Society. The meeting Friday night was large- ly social in its nature. Quartets from the Broadway Baptist Church and the First Church, North Side, assisted In a program of music. The organiza: tion sent a telegram to Henry F. Lackey, former president of the fed- pf Lackey, who now reside In El Paro.

headlines.title: FACES WHITE SLAVE CHARGE IN U. S. COURT
headlines.subtitle: Ludie Thomas Arnold Accused of Bringing Two Girls From North ta Dallas.
headlines.fullText: Thomas And was arraigned missioner Mitchell charged with white Www White Slavery in connection with bringing ona girl Band one 135 m and his bond was fixed at $450, in de- fault of which he went to the county It was agreed to bind him over for hearing before the Federal grand jury at the March term. The prisoner also is held on $2,000 bond on a similar charge Involving an Oklahoma girl.

headlines.title: TICKETSFOR AUDITORIUM DINNER READY FOR SALE
headlines.subtitle: One Dollar Price of Seat for Entertainment of Jan. 23.
headlines.fullText: Tickets for the Chamber of Com- merce auditorium celebration, Jan, 23, will be placed on male Monday morn- ing at the Chamber of Commerce on Sixth street, between Main and Com- merce streets. Tickets will be sold at $1. There are fourteen boxes in the auditorium with eight seats each, which will be sold at $16 per box. Box holders will be announced In the solety sections of the Sunday papers, Guy Richardson Pitner has charge of the musical program of the celebra- tion and win announce it during the weekt Burse of the best professional talent in the city will appear.

headlines.title: Phones for Fire Official; Entire Department Comes
headlines.subtitle: Relief Association Secretary Calls for Peninger and All Ap. paratus Runs to Office.
headlines.fullText: J. B. Rawlings, secretary of the Fort Worth Relief Association, took down the telephone receiver at his desk Sat- urday. "Lamar tiro six," quietly spoke the secretary "Fire station," came the quick re- "This is J. B. Rawlings, secretary "Can you tell me if A. W. Peninger, assistant state fire marshal is in town- He was informed that Peninger was out of the city, but that any Informa- tion he cared to leave would be delly- ered to Peninger immediately upon his arrival. But Rawlings had personal business and the receivers were hung Entire Department Calls. ina few minutes the entire Cen- tral fire station equipment dashed up in front of the Relief Association head- quarters at sixth and Throckmorton streets. The firemen Miffed around a few minutes and returned to the star "Bux-x-2-2-z, whir-r-r-r." whizzed the wheezy telephone on the secre- "Rawlings of the Fort Worth Rellet Association talking spoke Rawlings. debes, Did you turn in a fire alarm or is there a fire in your neighbor- hood?" Then They Laughed. Chief Bideker was informed that there had been no fire alarm given from the Relief Association and fur- ther that no one had talked on the association phone except Rawlings. himself, and that he had called cen- tral station to ask for Peninger. There was a laugh, then two laughs and Rawlings spoke. "I guess the treats are on you, chten; giggled Rawlings. "It's pretty 200 when a fellow asks for one man am draws the entire fire department."

headlines.title: FEW BIRTHS REPORTED
headlines.subtitle: Only Three Outside of City in County in December.
headlines.fullText: Only three births in Tarrant county reported to County Clerk Beavers dur Ing December, while seventy were re- One hundred and seven deaths were reported in the city and eleven in the county. One monthly report was filed Hat- It shows a total a las deaths for fire residents, There was a total of GR births. headlines.title: Uncle Tom Maben Again Candidate for Justice of Peace
headlines.fullText: "Uncle Tom" Maben has changed his mind, and Saturday, announced term as justice of the peace in place He previously had announced that he would quit official life and try being a private citizen. At that time his clerk, Wallace R. (Pipe) Standley. announced as a candidate to succeed Maben. Justice Maben has served twenty- one years as a public office holder. He was deputy sheriff in Marshall coun- ty. Tenm., for four years during his Thirty-four years ago he came to Fort Worth, Nineteen years ago he was appointed on the police force and Te then was elected constable and He served twe terms. He then retired and Southwestern Telephone and Tele- graph Company He then ran for justice of the peace and has continually held that office since that time.

headlines.title: BUILDERS CURBED IN USE OF BUSY STREETS
headlines.subtitle: Temporary Closing of Sidewalk Alone Permitted Under Amendment. Overhead Shield Required.
headlines.fullText: An amendment to the present street ordinance regarding the blockading of Main and Houston streets when build- ARE IN in progress will go before Tues- day" mension of The commander The amendment in brief cancels the of the street may hermann construction and permits instead tem- porary closing only of the sidewalk until excavation excavation must then be bridged over and the sidewalk be protected by over- head timbers, Placing of the upon protecting timbers anne fuels the street line is forbidden.

headlines.title: NEGRESS ARRESTED AT MEMPHIS WANTED HERI
headlines.subtitle: Lillian Brady Held in Connection With $3,000 Diamond Theft in Rooming House.
headlines.fullText: Lillian Brady, negress, wanted by th Fort Worth police in connection the theft of diamondsand from Della Murdock mah beer 906 Calhoun reste pord to word received Saturday by De tective Chief Connelley. City Detective Harmon left Satur day to bring the negress back to For Worth. The woman was employed & maid at the Murdock rooming house.

headlines.title: EFFECT OF HEREDITY SHOWN BY EXAMPLE
headlines.subtitle: Two Sets of Descendante of Same Man Will Be Discussed at Race Bet-
headlines.fullText: BATTLE CREEK, Mich. Jan. 2. Among the Interesting discussions at interruptions Sonra mace betterment here, Jan. 5 to. 12, will bene setter who will er Martin Kallikak and his two sets of children Kallikak, who was a person of some prominence, became the father of an Illegitimate son about the beginning of the Revolutionary war. The mother was a feeble-minded . woman whose name has been lost. From the son - descended in direct line 450 Individuals only 43 of whom have been normal, healthy and mane men and women. After the war Kallikak married a woman of good stock and from this union there have descended 176 in- dividuals, all except three of whom have been normal in body and mind. To discourage such unions as Kal- likak's first and encourage such as his second is the task undertaken by the conference.

headlines.title: INSURANCE KEY RATE CUT MAY BE SOUGHT
headlines.subtitle: Good Fire Records Frem Towns of the State Will Mean Effort Tsy at Reduction.
headlines.fullText: AUSTIN, Jan. 5. Good fire records for 1913 may result in the key rate Ter ming materially, reduced Texas. and indications now point many good records, according to the Texas insurance commission. About two weeks ago the commis- mon we go of the vari- 1913, showing the net premiums paid and losses sustained. Quite a num- ber have complied, showing flatter- ing records, The 1912 record will be compared with that of the two previous years, and then the commission will ascet" tain the extent of credits that may be given and thus reduce the key rate,

headlines.title: WOUNDED BY BANDITS, DETROIT OFFICER DIES
headlines.subtitle: Six of Seven Men Wounded While on Duty Have Died as Result.
headlines.fullText: DETROIT, Mich., Jan. 3-Patrol- man John Gordon died today of In- Juries received carly this morning when he was shot by bandits. Of seven Detroit officera shot while on duty since last March, six either were killed outright or dled as the re- entt of their intarle
